is_p_falciparum.evidence: Study of Plasmodium falciparum pfcrt codon 76 in 111 Cameroonian isolates.

reports_target_markers.evidence: Analyzes pfcrt codon 76 (Lys76Thr) and pfmdr1 codon 86 — both target markers.

reports_extractable_frequencies.evidence: Figure 1 shows distribution of 93 isolates by wild type/mutant/mixed pfcrt allele; counts of discordant isolates given, but overall precise numerator/denominator per allele is not clearly tabulated for the full 111 sample set.

sub_country_location.evidence: All samples from patients residing in Yaoundé (city, sub-national) — recruited at Nlongkak Catholic missionary dispensary.

temporal_resolution_within_3y.evidence: Samples collected from randomized clinical trials in Yaoundé 'from 1994 through 1998' — a 5-year span, and frequencies are pooled across this whole period with no per-year or <=3-year breakdown.

african_field_samples.evidence: Human clinical isolates from patients with acute uncomplicated malaria residing in Yaoundé, Cameroon.

is_primary_study.evidence: Primary empirical study analyzing 111 field isolates by PCR-RFLP.

duplicate_risk.evidence: 'all available polymorphism and phenotype correlational data from our study site since 1994 were recapitulated' and refs 7-9,13 describe overlapping pfmdr1/cg2 characterization of the same Cameroonian isolates in prior publications.

supplement.evidence: No supplementary materials referenced; frequency data are in main text/Figure 1.

assessment.summary: Primary study of P. falciparum pfcrt codon 76 (and pfmdr1 86) in 111 clinical isolates from Yaoundé, Cameroon, collected 1994-1998, correlating with in vivo/in vitro chloroquine response. Sub-country location (Yaoundé) is clear, but frequencies are pooled over a 5-year collection span with no finer temporal breakdown, failing the <=3-year resolution criterion. High duplicate risk as data overlap prior publications from the same study site.
exclusion_reasons: Frequencies pooled across a 5-year collection window (1994-1998) with no per-year or <=3-year temporal breakdown
High risk of overlap with previously published data from the same Yaoundé study site (refs 7-9, 13)
